title: The Friction Is A Feature, Not A Bug: Teaching and Mentoring in the Age of AI
excerpt: Those who have been following me for a while will know that teaching and mentoring are a Big Deal™️...
content_html:
Those who have been following me for a while will know that teaching and mentoring are a Big Deal™️ to me.

Before I got into tech I was a teacher, and still consider myself a teacher at heart. Being a teacher and mentor was never separate in my eyes from being a good programmer and engineer; on the contrary, teaching was a tool that helped me become better at my craft at every stage of the journey.

But in the last few years, and accelerating in the last few months, the landscape for teaching and learning has been changing at a scary pace. The advent of LLMs and "AI" coding assistants has drastically shifted how we acquire engineering skills in ways that we are definitely not prepared for.

All of that has prompted many thoughts and conversations, and I hope to distill some of them in this blog post.

In true Talmudic fashion, this post doesn't contain too many answers and will hopefully leave you with more questions than you started with. But asking the questions is how we start these conversations, and these conversations need to be happening if we are to do right by the coming generation of programmers and engineers.

Don't Spoon-Feed Me

As a student, whether in Yeshiva when I used to spend hours each day poring over dense Talmudic legal debates and esoteric Chassidic philosophy or later while learning Rails and React at the Flatiron bootcamp, I quickly realized an uncomfortable truth about skill acquisition.

My best, most profound learning never happened when a lesson went smoothly; it happened when I was painfully stuck, banging my head against a cryptic error message or wrestling with a concept that just wouldn't click (usually at 2 AM, fueled by cold coffee and sheer stubbornness).

When you strip away that struggle, you get rid of the growth. And when you get rid of the growth, the learning just doesn't happen.

Even if you memorize just enough to pass the test, "easy come easy go." Without that cognitive friction, the knowledge evaporates the moment you close your editor.

The best teachers and mentors I ever had understood this intuitively. When I was staring at a broken script knowing there was probably a stupidly easy fix, they didn’t just swoop in and drop the solution onto my screen (much to the vocal frustration of my sleep-deprived brain).

Instead, they wielded tools like the Socratic method. They would point to a specific line of code and start asking gentle, probing questions to expose my flawed assumptions.

They actively leaned into the friction to create just enough cognitive space for me to connect the dots on my own.

They understood a fundamental rule of our teaching craft: that good mentorship is not about getting to the code. The goal wasn't just to get the tests turning green today; it was to build the mental resilience required to debug the next inevitable failure tomorrow.

And it was these skills that I took with. me when it was my turn to become the teacher, whether it was teaching second graders at the Jewish Online School, as a TA at Flatiron, or when mentoring new teammates joining my team.

The Yes-Man in Your IDE

Fast forward to today, and our industry has largely outsourced that initial layer of mentorship to LLMs and coding assistants. And here is the uncomfortable truth: AI makes a terrible Socratic tutor.

If you’ve spent any serious time pairing (or even just chatting) with modern LLMs, you know they are pathological yes-men. Tell an AI you want to architect a distributed microservice mesh for a todo-list app with twelve active users, and it won't push back. It will chime in with "That is a brilliant insight!" and instantly spit out 400 lines of boilerplate.

AI defaults to blind trust. It rarely challenges a mistaken assumption, and when you ask it why a stack trace is blowing up, it immediately serves up a copypastable fix instead of helping you understand the underlying bug.

By acting like an algorithmic vending machine (error_in => fix_out), AI systematically removes all the cognitive friction from the development process.

In our rush for 10x velocity, we forgot a foundational rule of skill acquisition: friction is a feature, not a bug. That was a load-bearing friction we removed!

This isn't just philosophical speculation; there's hard data to back it up. A recent study by Anthropic (or dive into the raw paper here if you love parsing methodology) took 52 software engineers learning an unfamiliar programming library and split them into two groups: one with AI coding assistants, and one coding by hand.

The overall results were as damning as they were not surprising. The engineers who delegated their coding to AI suffered a 17% drop in conceptual mastery and debugging skills (the equivalent of two full letter grades!) often without completing their tasks meaningfully faster than the manual control group.

Why? Because when you offload the cognitive friction of getting painfully stuck, your brain never builds the deep neural pathways required to evaluate whether the generated code is actually any good.

Now, to be fair, there was some nuance in the data: a handful of the AI-assisted engineers actually outperformed the manual control group. But who were they? They were the developers who refused to trust the AI blindly. They treated the LLM like a study partner, asking probing follow-up questions and manually injecting their own friction back into the process.

For everyone else, the automated yes-man just quietly destroyed their understanding.

The Learner’s Dilemma: Manually Injecting Friction

This dynamic leaves junior developers in a massive bind.

Friends and mentees who are earlier in their career have complained that they feel stuck in a frustrating Catch-22:

On one hand, they have to pragmatically accept the reality that AI isn't going anywhere. If an LLM can generate working boilerplate from a single prompt, you can't just stubborn your way out of using it. Ignoring AI entirely feels irresponsible, considering that you will be using these tools heavily in your day-to-day workflows.

But on the other hand, if you rely on that autocomplete blindly, you get trapped in what's known as "superficial learning." You get a quick answer that allows you to pass a test suite or get a PR approved without truly understanding the mechanics under the hood. You miss out on the "deep learning" where the actual, career-sustaining growth happens.

In my experience, that superficiality is a direct pipeline to developer anxiety and impostor syndrome. True professional confidence doesn't come from hitting Tab to accept a prompt; it comes from code ownership and intense intellectual toil and sweat.

To survive this, diligent learners are now being forced to manually inject friction back into their workflows just to keep their brains from turning off.

A junior developer friend of mine has resorted to explicitly prompting their IDE with guardrail instructions like: "I want to learn how to code it myself, rather than copying and pasting, so enable that however you can."

They take notes by hand (yes, actual paper and pen!) and set physical timers just to recreate the safeguards of active listening that a tool designed for instant gratification systematically tries to bypass.

Why We Need the Messy Web of Context

This need for self-imposed struggle makes total sense when you look at Emily Bender and Alex Hanna’s book, The AI Con, which highlights why friction in information access is actually a feature, not a bug.

Think about what happens when you manually hunt down an obscure bug using a search engine.

You read messy documentation, scan through twelve-comment GitHub issue threads (usually ending with a maintainer closing it as "not a bug" in 2014), weigh the credibility of conflicting answers on Stack Overflow, and stumble across related gotchas along the way.

That messy web of meta-information is where real engineering context is built.

An AI chatbot flattens all of that rich, frustrating struggle into a single, authoritative-sounding (and occasionally "hallucinated") summary. It gives you the answer to your immediate problem while robbing your brain of the surrounding ecosystem knowledge required to solve the next one.

The Chavrusa Model: Pair Programming && The Adversarial Rubber Duck

This brings me to an ancient pedagogical model that survives to this day in Yeshivas (Talmudic academies) around the world, and one that software engineering desperately needs right about now.

In a traditional Yeshiva, you rarely see people sitting in silent isolation reading a book or passively listening to a lecture. Instead, the room is loud, energetic, and filled with people arguing fiercely in pairs.

This study partner system is known as a Chavrusa (from the Aramaic word for "friend" or "companion").

When you learn with a Chavrusa, your partner isn't there to read the text to you, nor do they nod along and sycophantically affirm how brilliant your theories are. A real Chavrusa regularly challenges your logic, pokes holes in your arguments, pushees back against shaky assumptions, and generally refuses to let you get away with intellectual laziness.

You don't master the Talmud by reading cliff notes or listening to a brilliant lecture (though there's some of that as well); you master it through Yegiah (a Hebrew term that translates to intense intellectual toil and sweat).

If you think about it (and as I mentioned in my 2021 RailsConf talk), pair programming is just the tech industry's recreation of the Chavrusa.

And here is the most important part of that system: it's not only the junior member of the pair who's learning in a true Chavrusa.

It doesn't matter if you are pairing with a 10-year staff engineer or a junior developer who just finished a bootcamp; the learning is always a two-way street!

When your partner acts as an adversarial rubber duck (one that actually talks back!), they force you to slow down, articulate your hidden assumptions, and defend your code logic from start to finish. Both of you gain from that friction, and both of you walk away with a significantly better mental model than you started with.

Rabbi Ḥama, son of Rabbi Ḥanina, said: What is the meaning of that which is written: “Iron sharpens iron, so a man sharpens the countenance of his friend” (Proverbs 27:17)? This verse comes to tell you that just as with iron blades, one sharpens the other when they are rubbed against each other, so too, when Torah scholars study together, they sharpen one another.

-Ta'anit 7a

In our rush for 10x velocity, we tried to replace human pair programming with AI copilots. But in doing so, we forgot a critical dependency: we exchanged the adversarial Chavrusa and Yegiah for a sycophantic robo-duck.

Empathetic Balancing Act

A common response I hear from the tech crowd is: "Well, let's just fix this with better system prompts! Let's build guardrails that instruct the AI to act like a Socratic tutor instead of just giving away the answer!"

There are plenty of tools attempting this right now, but I am deeply skeptical that technical guardrails can solve a fundamentally human problem.

First of all, relying on a prompt to withhold answers requires elite learner discipline. Some students can stick to it, but that level of willpower is rare, especially when your cognitive battery is hitting empty.

When a junior developer is staring at a cryptic NullPointerException at 2 AM and their brain is fried, a prompt asking them to "reflect on the lifecycle of the object" is getting bypassed immediately. If all it takes is typing /solution or opening a new browser tab to get the dopamine hit of copypastable code, not too many people I know will power through.

More importantly, LLMs are fundamentally architected to please. Even if you prompt-engineer them to be tough Socratic tutors, their core tuning means that the moment a user expresses genuine frustration, the AI folds like a cheap lawn chair and hands over the syntax anyway.

Why? Because an LLM lacks the quality most important in an educator: empathy.

Mentorship is a human pedagogical balancing act. A great mentor understands the fine line between productive struggle and demoralizing suffering. A good educator can read the room. They notice the heavy sighs, the mounting panic, and the sheer exhaustion.

Sure, as a student I learned best when I was given the tools to figure things out on my own. But sometimes I maxed out my energy on the stupid error message, and all of the leading questions my tutors were asking me felt like they were just causing me to circle and circle around the one insight that would make it all work, an insight my brain just couldn't reach.

That is the point a good teacher knows to step back, smile, say "Hey, JavaScript is just weird sometimes," and hand the exact line of code I needed to get unstuck so I could finally go get some sleep.

An algorithm cannot read a learner's emotional state. It cannot dynamically toggle between a challenging sparring partner and a supportive safety net. Code can never replicate that relationship.

The Unsolved Catch-22

So where does this leave us? If I’m being entirely honest, I have way more questions than answers right now (not exactly what you want to hear from a tech blog, I know!).

We are sitting on a massive, industry-wide Catch-22:

On one hand, it would be borderline professional malpractice to forbid junior developers from using AI. It is the modern IDE (which, back when I was learning to code, gatekeepers argued juniors shouldn't use either—I disagreed then and I disagree now). If juniors will be expected to use these tools on the job, it hardly makes sense not to train them to use them effectively.

On the other hand, the engineers getting that massive 10x velocity boost without introducing architecture-destroying bugs are the seniors who spent years writing code without it.

We know when an LLM is spinning its wheels on a false premise because we have a built-in BS-meter, hard-earned through years of manual debugging.

How does someone without that pre-AI experience build that intuition today? They won't get it from a sycophantic yes-man chat-bot assistant. And manually inventing your own cognitive friction requires a level of self-discipline that is hard to maintain on a tight deadline.

Maybe the future of onboarding relies on doubling down on human pair programming—having juniors shadow seniors for months on end to soak up that pre-AI intuition by proxy. After all, pair programming has always been one of the best onboarding methods, even before Claude. But not all companies are interested in the investment that full-time pairing requires in an industry often obsessed with shipping yesterday.

What I do know is that we cannot afford to ignore the problem or assume that better LLM models will magically fix our talent pipeline.

If we just hand junior developers an oracle and fail to preserve the empathetic, rigorous struggle of the Chavrusa, we are going to wake up in a few years to a terrifying system error: an industry with a massive, AI-generated codebase to maintain, and not enough senior engineers left who know how to maintain it.

What are you and your teams doing to keep productive friction alive? I'd love to hear your thoughts.

sentences.zh: 作为一名学生，无论是在 Yeshiva 每天花几个小时钻研密集的塔木德法律辩论和深奥的 Chassisdic 哲学，还是后来在 Flatiron 训练营学习 Rails 和 React 时，我很快就意识到了关于技能获取的一个令人不安的事实。

blocks.html_en: My best, most profound learning never happened when a lesson went smoothly; it happened when I was painfully stuck, banging my head against a cryptic error message or wrestling with a concept that just wouldn't click (usually at 2 AM, fueled by cold coffee and sheer stubbornness).

sentences.en: My best, most profound learning never happened when a lesson went smoothly; it happened when I was painfully stuck, banging my head against a cryptic error message or wrestling with a concept that just wouldn't click (usually at 2 AM, fueled by cold coffee and sheer stubbornness).
sentences.zh: 我最好、最深刻的学习从来都不是在课程顺利的时候发生的；当我痛苦地陷入困境时，我的头撞在一个神秘的错误消息上，或者与一个根本无法理解的概念搏斗时（通常是在凌晨 2 点，由冷咖啡和纯粹的固执推动）。

blocks.html_en: When you strip away that struggle, you get rid of the growth. And when you get rid of the growth, the learning just doesn't happen.

sentences.en: When you strip away that struggle, you get rid of the growth.
sentences.zh: 当你消除这种挣扎时，你就摆脱了成长。

sentences.en: And when you get rid of the growth, the learning just doesn't happen.
sentences.zh: 当你摆脱成长时，学习就不会发生。

blocks.html_en: Even if you memorize just enough to pass the test, "easy come easy go." Without that cognitive friction, the knowledge evaporates the moment you close your editor.

sentences.en: Even if you memorize just enough to pass the test, "easy come easy go."
sentences.zh: 即使你的记忆力只够通过考试，“来得容易去得也快”。

sentences.en: Without that cognitive friction, the knowledge evaporates the moment you close your editor.
sentences.zh: 如果没有这种认知摩擦，当你关闭编辑器时，知识就会消失。

blocks.html_en: The best teachers and mentors I ever had understood this intuitively. When I was staring at a broken script knowing there was probably a stupidly easy fix, they didn’t just swoop in and drop the solution onto my screen (much to the vocal frustration of my sleep-deprived brain).

sentences.en: The best teachers and mentors I ever had understood this intuitively.
sentences.zh: 我所见过的最好的老师和导师直观地理解了这一点。

sentences.en: When I was staring at a broken script knowing there was probably a stupidly easy fix, they didn’t just swoop in and drop the solution onto my screen (much to the vocal frustration of my sleep-deprived brain).
sentences.zh: 当我盯着一个损坏的脚本，知道可能有一个简单到愚蠢的修复方法时，他们并没有突然冲进来，把解决方案放到我的屏幕上（这让我睡眠不足的大脑发出了沮丧的声音）。

blocks.html_en: Instead, they wielded tools like the Socratic method. They would point to a specific line of code and start asking gentle, probing questions to expose my flawed assumptions.

sentences.en: Instead, they wielded tools like the Socratic method.
sentences.zh: 相反，他们使用苏格拉底方法等工具。

sentences.en: They would point to a specific line of code and start asking gentle, probing questions to expose my flawed assumptions.
sentences.zh: 他们会指出某行特定的代码，并开始提出温和的、探究性的问题，以揭露我有缺陷的假设。

blocks.html_en: They actively leaned into the friction to create just enough cognitive space for me to connect the dots on my own.

sentences.en: They actively leaned into the friction to create just enough cognitive space for me to connect the dots on my own.
sentences.zh: 他们积极地利用摩擦来创造足够的认知空间，让我自己把这些点联系起来。

blocks.html_en: They understood a fundamental rule of our teaching craft: that good mentorship is not about getting to the code. The goal wasn't just to get the tests turning green today; it was to build the mental resilience required to debug the next inevitable failure tomorrow.

sentences.en: They understood a fundamental rule of our teaching craft: that good mentorship is not about getting to the code.
sentences.zh: 他们了解我们教学技巧的一条基本规则：良好的指导并不是要了解代码。

sentences.en: The goal wasn't just to get the tests turning green today; it was to build the mental resilience required to debug the next inevitable failure tomorrow.
sentences.zh: 今天的目标不仅仅是让测试变绿；这是为了建立解决明天下一个不可避免的故障所需的心理弹性。

blocks.html_en: And it was these skills that I took with. me when it was my turn to become the teacher, whether it was teaching second graders at the Jewish Online School, as a TA at Flatiron, or when mentoring new teammates joining my team.

sentences.en: And it was these skills that I took with.
sentences.zh: 我所掌握的正是这些技能。

sentences.en: me when it was my turn to become the teacher, whether it was teaching second graders at the Jewish Online School, as a TA at Flatiron, or when mentoring new teammates joining my team.
sentences.zh: 当轮到我成为老师时，无论是在犹太在线学校教二年级学生，在熨斗公司担任助教，还是在指导加入我的团队的新队友时。

blocks.html_en: The Yes-Man in Your IDE

sentences.en: The Yes-Man in Your IDE
sentences.zh: IDE 中的唯唯诺诺的人

blocks.html_en: Fast forward to today, and our industry has largely outsourced that initial layer of mentorship to LLMs and coding assistants. And here is the uncomfortable truth: AI makes a terrible Socratic tutor.

sentences.en: Fast forward to today, and our industry has largely outsourced that initial layer of mentorship to LLMs and coding assistants.
sentences.zh: 快进到今天，我们的行业已将最初的指导层很大程度上外包给了法学硕士和编码助理。

sentences.en: And here is the uncomfortable truth: AI makes a terrible Socratic tutor.
sentences.zh: 这是一个令人不安的事实：人工智能会成为一个糟糕的苏格拉底式导师。

blocks.html_en: If you’ve spent any serious time pairing (or even just chatting) with modern LLMs, you know they are pathological yes-men. Tell an AI you want to architect a distributed microservice mesh for a todo-list app with twelve active users, and it won't push back. It will chime in with "That is a brilliant insight!" and instantly spit out 400 lines of boilerplate.

sentences.en: If you’ve spent any serious time pairing (or even just chatting) with modern LLMs, you know they are pathological yes-men.
sentences.zh: 如果你认真地花时间与现代法学硕士结对（甚至只是聊天），你就会知道他们是病态的唯唯诺诺的人。

sentences.en: Tell an AI you want to architect a distributed microservice mesh for a todo-list app with twelve active users, and it won't push back.
sentences.zh: 告诉人工智能你想为一个有 12 个活跃用户的待办事项列表应用程序构建一个分布式微服务网格，它不会拒绝。

sentences.en: It will chime in with "That is a brilliant insight!"
sentences.zh: 它将附和“这是一个绝妙的见解！”

sentences.en: and instantly spit out 400 lines of boilerplate.
sentences.zh: 并立即吐出 400 行样板文件。

blocks.html_en: AI defaults to blind trust. It rarely challenges a mistaken assumption, and when you ask it why a stack trace is blowing up, it immediately serves up a copypastable fix instead of helping you understand the underlying bug.

sentences.en: AI defaults to blind trust.
sentences.zh: 人工智能默认是盲目信任。

sentences.en: It rarely challenges a mistaken assumption, and when you ask it why a stack trace is blowing up, it immediately serves up a copypastable fix instead of helping you understand the underlying bug.
sentences.zh: 它很少挑战错误的假设，当你问它为什么堆栈跟踪会崩溃时，它会立即提供可复制粘贴的修复程序，而不是帮助你理解潜在的错误。

blocks.html_en: By acting like an algorithmic vending machine (error_in => fix_out), AI systematically removes all the cognitive friction from the development process.

sentences.en: By acting like an algorithmic vending machine (error_in => fix_out), AI systematically removes all the cognitive friction from the development process.
sentences.zh: 通过像算法自动售货机（error_in => fix_out）一样，人工智能系统地消除了开发过程中的所有认知摩擦。

blocks.html_en: In our rush for 10x velocity, we forgot a foundational rule of skill acquisition: friction is a feature, not a bug. That was a load-bearing friction we removed!

sentences.en: In our rush for 10x velocity, we forgot a foundational rule of skill acquisition: friction is a feature, not a bug.
sentences.zh: 在我们追求 10 倍速度的过程中，我们忘记了技能获取的基本规则：摩擦是一种功能，而不是错误。

sentences.en: That was a load-bearing friction we removed!
sentences.zh: 那是我们移除的承重摩擦！

blocks.html_en: This isn't just philosophical speculation; there's hard data to back it up. A recent study by Anthropic (or dive into the raw paper here if you love parsing methodology) took 52 software engineers learning an unfamiliar programming library and split them into two groups: one with AI coding assistants, and one coding by hand.

sentences.en: This isn't just philosophical speculation; there's hard data to back it up.
sentences.zh: 这不仅仅是哲学推测；有硬数据支持它。

sentences.en: A recent study by Anthropic (or dive into the raw paper here if you love parsing methodology) took 52 software engineers learning an unfamiliar programming library and split them into two groups: one with AI coding assistants, and one coding by hand.
sentences.zh: Anthropic 最近的一项研究（如果您喜欢解析方法，请深入了解这里的原始论文）让 52 名软件工程师学习一个不熟悉的编程库，并将他们分成两组：一组使用 AI 编码助手，一组手动编码。

blocks.html_en: The overall results were as damning as they were not surprising. The engineers who delegated their coding to AI suffered a 17% drop in conceptual mastery and debugging skills (the equivalent of two full letter grades!) often without completing their tasks meaningfully faster than the manual control group.

sentences.en: The overall results were as damning as they were not surprising.
sentences.zh: 总体结果既令人震惊，又不足为奇。

sentences.en: The engineers who delegated their coding to AI suffered a 17% drop in conceptual mastery and debugging skills (the equivalent of two full letter grades!)
sentences.zh: 将编码委托给 AI 的工程师的概念掌握和调试技能下降了 17%（相当于两个完整的字母等级！）

sentences.en: often without completing their tasks meaningfully faster than the manual control group.
sentences.zh: 通常无法比手动对照组更快地完成任务。

blocks.html_en: Why? Because when you offload the cognitive friction of getting painfully stuck, your brain never builds the deep neural pathways required to evaluate whether the generated code is actually any good.

sentences.en: Why?
sentences.zh: 为什么？

sentences.en: Because when you offload the cognitive friction of getting painfully stuck, your brain never builds the deep neural pathways required to evaluate whether the generated code is actually any good.
sentences.zh: 因为当你摆脱了痛苦地卡住的认知摩擦时，你的大脑永远不会构建评估生成的代码是否真的有任何好处所需的深层神经通路。

blocks.html_en: Now, to be fair, there was some nuance in the data: a handful of the AI-assisted engineers actually outperformed the manual control group. But who were they? They were the developers who refused to trust the AI blindly. They treated the LLM like a study partner, asking probing follow-up questions and manually injecting their own friction back into the process.

sentences.en: Now, to be fair, there was some nuance in the data: a handful of the AI-assisted engineers actually outperformed the manual control group.
sentences.zh: 现在，公平地说，数据中存在一些细微差别：少数人工智能辅助工程师的表现实际上优于手动控制组。

sentences.en: But who were they?
sentences.zh: 但他们是谁？

sentences.en: They were the developers who refused to trust the AI blindly.
sentences.zh: 他们是拒绝盲目相信人工智能的开发者。

sentences.en: They treated the LLM like a study partner, asking probing follow-up questions and manually injecting their own friction back into the process.
sentences.zh: 他们像对待学习伙伴一样对待法学硕士，提出探究性的后续问题，并手动将自己的摩擦注入到这个过程中。

blocks.html_en: For everyone else, the automated yes-man just quietly destroyed their understanding.

sentences.en: For everyone else, the automated yes-man just quietly destroyed their understanding.
sentences.zh: 对于其他人来说，自动化的唯唯诺诺的人只是悄悄地破坏了他们的理解。

blocks.html_en: The Learner’s Dilemma: Manually Injecting Friction

sentences.en: The Learner’s Dilemma: Manually Injecting Friction
sentences.zh: 学习者的困境：手动注入摩擦力

blocks.html_en: This dynamic leaves junior developers in a massive bind.

sentences.en: This dynamic leaves junior developers in a massive bind.
sentences.zh: 这种动态让初级开发人员陷入了巨大的困境。

blocks.html_en: Friends and mentees who are earlier in their career have complained that they feel stuck in a frustrating Catch-22:

sentences.en: Friends and mentees who are earlier in their career have complained that they feel stuck in a frustrating Catch-22:
sentences.zh: 职业生涯早期的朋友和学员抱怨说，他们感觉陷入了令人沮丧的第 22 条军规：

blocks.html_en: On one hand, they have to pragmatically accept the reality that AI isn't going anywhere. If an LLM can generate working boilerplate from a single prompt, you can't just stubborn your way out of using it. Ignoring AI entirely feels irresponsible, considering that you will be using these tools heavily in your day-to-day workflows.

sentences.en: On one hand, they have to pragmatically accept the reality that AI isn't going anywhere.
sentences.zh: 一方面，他们必须务实地接受人工智能不会消失的现实。

sentences.en: If an LLM can generate working boilerplate from a single prompt, you can't just stubborn your way out of using it.
sentences.zh: 如果法学硕士可以通过单个提示生成工作样板，那么您就不能固执地不使用它。

sentences.en: Ignoring AI entirely feels irresponsible, considering that you will be using these tools heavily in your day-to-day workflows.
sentences.zh: 考虑到您将在日常工作流程中大量使用这些工具，忽略人工智能完全让人感觉不负责任。

blocks.html_en: But on the other hand, if you rely on that autocomplete blindly, you get trapped in what's known as "superficial learning." You get a quick answer that allows you to pass a test suite or get a PR approved without truly understanding the mechanics under the hood. You miss out on the "deep learning" where the actual, career-sustaining growth happens.

sentences.en: But on the other hand, if you rely on that autocomplete blindly, you get trapped in what's known as "superficial learning."
sentences.zh: 但另一方面，如果你盲目依赖自动完成功能，你就会陷入所谓的“肤浅学习”。

sentences.en: You get a quick answer that allows you to pass a test suite or get a PR approved without truly understanding the mechanics under the hood.
sentences.zh: 您会得到一个快速答案，允许您通过测试套件或获得 PR 批准，而无需真正了解底层机制。

sentences.en: You miss out on the "deep learning" where the actual, career-sustaining growth happens.
sentences.zh: 你错过了“深度学习”，而真正的、职业维持性的成长正是在“深度学习”中发生的。

blocks.html_en: In my experience, that superficiality is a direct pipeline to developer anxiety and impostor syndrome. True professional confidence doesn't come from hitting Tab to accept a prompt; it comes from code ownership and intense intellectual toil and sweat.

sentences.en: In my experience, that superficiality is a direct pipeline to developer anxiety and impostor syndrome.
sentences.zh: 根据我的经验，这种肤浅是导致开发人员焦虑和冒名顶替综合症的直接途径。

sentences.en: True professional confidence doesn't come from hitting Tab to accept a prompt; it comes from code ownership and intense intellectual toil and sweat.
sentences.zh: 真正的职业信心不是来自于按 Tab 键接受提示；而是来自于按 Tab 键接受提示。它来自代码所有权和大量的智力劳动和汗水。

blocks.html_en: To survive this, diligent learners are now being forced to manually inject friction back into their workflows just to keep their brains from turning off.

sentences.en: To survive this, diligent learners are now being forced to manually inject friction back into their workflows just to keep their brains from turning off.
sentences.zh: 为了生存下去，勤奋的学习者现在被迫手动将摩擦注入到他们的工作流程中，以防止他们的大脑关闭。

blocks.html_en: A junior developer friend of mine has resorted to explicitly prompting their IDE with guardrail instructions like: "I want to learn how to code it myself, rather than copying and pasting, so enable that however you can."

sentences.en: A junior developer friend of mine has resorted to explicitly prompting their IDE with guardrail instructions like: "I want to learn how to code it myself, rather than copying and pasting, so enable that however you can."
sentences.zh: 我的一位初级开发人员朋友使用护栏指令明确提示他们的 IDE，例如：“我想学习如何自己编码，而不是复制和粘贴，因此请尽可能启用它。”

blocks.html_en: They take notes by hand (yes, actual paper and pen!) and set physical timers just to recreate the safeguards of active listening that a tool designed for instant gratification systematically tries to bypass.

sentences.en: They take notes by hand (yes, actual paper and pen!)
sentences.zh: 他们用手记笔记（是的，真正的纸和笔！）

sentences.en: and set physical timers just to recreate the safeguards of active listening that a tool designed for instant gratification systematically tries to bypass.
sentences.zh: 并设置物理计时器只是为了重新创建积极倾听的保障，而为即时满足而设计的工具会系统地试图绕过这一保障。

blocks.html_en: Why We Need the Messy Web of Context

sentences.en: Why We Need the Messy Web of Context
sentences.zh: 为什么我们需要混乱的上下文网络

blocks.html_en: This need for self-imposed struggle makes total sense when you look at Emily Bender and Alex Hanna’s book, The AI Con, which highlights why friction in information access is actually a feature, not a bug.

sentences.en: This need for self-imposed struggle makes total sense when you look at Emily Bender and Alex Hanna’s book, The AI Con, which highlights why friction in information access is actually a feature, not a bug.
sentences.zh: 当你读一下 Emily Bender 和 Alex Hanna 的书《The AI Con》时，这种自我斗争的需要就完全有意义了，这本书强调了为什么信息访问中的摩擦实际上是一个功能，而不是一个错误。

blocks.html_en: Think about what happens when you manually hunt down an obscure bug using a search engine.

sentences.en: Think about what happens when you manually hunt down an obscure bug using a search engine.
sentences.zh: 想想当您使用搜索引擎手动寻找一个不起眼的错误时会发生什么。

blocks.html_en: You read messy documentation, scan through twelve-comment GitHub issue threads (usually ending with a maintainer closing it as "not a bug" in 2014), weigh the credibility of conflicting answers on Stack Overflow, and stumble across related gotchas along the way.

sentences.en: You read messy documentation, scan through twelve-comment GitHub issue threads (usually ending with a maintainer closing it as "not a bug" in 2014), weigh the credibility of conflicting answers on Stack Overflow, and stumble across related gotchas along the way.
sentences.zh: 你阅读凌乱的文档，浏览 12 个评论的 GitHub 问题线程（通常以维护者在 2014 年将其关闭为“不是错误”而结束），权衡 Stack Overflow 上相互冲突的答案的可信度，并一路上偶然发现相关的问题。

blocks.html_en: That messy web of meta-information is where real engineering context is built.

sentences.en: That messy web of meta-information is where real engineering context is built.
sentences.zh: 混乱的元信息网络是构建真正的工程环境的地方。

blocks.html_en: An AI chatbot flattens all of that rich, frustrating struggle into a single, authoritative-sounding (and occasionally "hallucinated") summary. It gives you the answer to your immediate problem while robbing your brain of the surrounding ecosystem knowledge required to solve the next one.

sentences.en: An AI chatbot flattens all of that rich, frustrating struggle into a single, authoritative-sounding (and occasionally "hallucinated") summary.
sentences.zh: 人工智能聊天机器人将所有丰富的、令人沮丧的斗争简化为一个听起来很权威（有时是“幻觉”）的摘要。

sentences.en: It gives you the answer to your immediate problem while robbing your brain of the surrounding ecosystem knowledge required to solve the next one.
sentences.zh: 它为你提供了当前问题的答案，同时剥夺了你的大脑解决下一个问题所需的周围生态系统知识。

blocks.html_en: The Chavrusa Model: Pair Programming && The Adversarial Rubber Duck

sentences.en: The Chavrusa Model: Pair Programming && The Adversarial Rubber Duck
sentences.zh: Chavrusa 模型：结对编程和对抗性橡皮鸭

blocks.html_en: This brings me to an ancient pedagogical model that survives to this day in Yeshivas (Talmudic academies) around the world, and one that software engineering desperately needs right about now.

sentences.en: This brings me to an ancient pedagogical model that survives to this day in Yeshivas (Talmudic academies) around the world, and one that software engineering desperately needs right about now.
sentences.zh: 这让我想到了一种古老的教学模型，该模型至今仍在世界各地的 Yeshivas（塔木德学院）中幸存，也是软件工程现在迫切需要的模型。

blocks.html_en: In a traditional Yeshiva, you rarely see people sitting in silent isolation reading a book or passively listening to a lecture. Instead, the room is loud, energetic, and filled with people arguing fiercely in pairs.

sentences.en: In a traditional Yeshiva, you rarely see people sitting in silent isolation reading a book or passively listening to a lecture.
sentences.zh: 在传统的犹太学院中，你很少看到人们安静地坐着看书或被动地听讲座。

sentences.en: Instead, the room is loud, energetic, and filled with people arguing fiercely in pairs.
sentences.zh: 相反，房间里很吵闹，充满活力，充满了成双成对地激烈争吵的人。

blocks.html_en: This study partner system is known as a Chavrusa (from the Aramaic word for "friend" or "companion").

sentences.en: This study partner system is known as a Chavrusa (from the Aramaic word for "friend" or "companion").
sentences.zh: 这个学习伙伴系统被称为 Chavrusa（源自亚拉姆语，意为“朋友”或“同伴”）。

blocks.html_en: When you learn with a Chavrusa, your partner isn't there to read the text to you, nor do they nod along and sycophantically affirm how brilliant your theories are. A real Chavrusa regularly challenges your logic, pokes holes in your arguments, pushees back against shaky assumptions, and generally refuses to let you get away with intellectual laziness.

sentences.en: When you learn with a Chavrusa, your partner isn't there to read the text to you, nor do they nod along and sycophantically affirm how brilliant your theories are.
sentences.zh: 当你和 Chavrusa 一起学习时，你的搭档不会在那里读课文给你听，也不会点头同意并阿谀奉承地肯定你的理论有多么出色。

sentences.en: A real Chavrusa regularly challenges your logic, pokes holes in your arguments, pushees back against shaky assumptions, and generally refuses to let you get away with intellectual laziness.
sentences.zh: 真正的查夫鲁萨经常挑战你的逻辑，在你的论点中找出漏洞，反对不可靠的假设，并且通常拒绝让你摆脱智力上的懒惰。

blocks.html_en: You don't master the Talmud by reading cliff notes or listening to a brilliant lecture (though there's some of that as well); you master it through Yegiah (a Hebrew term that translates to intense intellectual toil and sweat).

sentences.en: You don't master the Talmud by reading cliff notes or listening to a brilliant lecture (though there's some of that as well); you master it through Yegiah (a Hebrew term that translates to intense intellectual toil and sweat).
sentences.zh: 你不能通过阅读悬崖笔记或听精彩的讲座来掌握《塔木德》（尽管也有一些这样的方法）；你可以通过 Yegiah（希伯来语术语，翻译过来就是大量的智力劳动和汗水）来掌握它。

blocks.html_en: If you think about it (and as I mentioned in my 2021 RailsConf talk), pair programming is just the tech industry's recreation of the Chavrusa.

sentences.en: If you think about it (and as I mentioned in my 2021 RailsConf talk), pair programming is just the tech industry's recreation of the Chavrusa.
sentences.zh: 如果你仔细想想（正如我在 2021 年 RailsConf 演讲中提到的），结对编程只是科技行业对 Chavrusa 的再创造。

blocks.html_en: And here is the most important part of that system: it's not only the junior member of the pair who's learning in a true Chavrusa.

sentences.en: And here is the most important part of that system: it's not only the junior member of the pair who's learning in a true Chavrusa.
sentences.zh: 这是该系统中最重要的部分：不仅仅是两人中的初级成员在真正的 Chavrusa 中学习。

blocks.html_en: It doesn't matter if you are pairing with a 10-year staff engineer or a junior developer who just finished a bootcamp; the learning is always a two-way street!

sentences.en: It doesn't matter if you are pairing with a 10-year staff engineer or a junior developer who just finished a bootcamp; the learning is always a two-way street!
sentences.zh: 无论您是与工作 10 年的资深工程师还是刚刚完成训练营的初级开发人员结对，都没关系；学习永远是一条双向路！

blocks.html_en: When your partner acts as an adversarial rubber duck (one that actually talks back!), they force you to slow down, articulate your hidden assumptions, and defend your code logic from start to finish. Both of you gain from that friction, and both of you walk away with a significantly better mental model than you started with.

sentences.en: When your partner acts as an adversarial rubber duck (one that actually talks back!), they force you to slow down, articulate your hidden assumptions, and defend your code logic from start to finish.
sentences.zh: 当你的伙伴扮演一只敌对的橡皮鸭（实际上是顶嘴的橡皮鸭！）时，他们会迫使你放慢速度，阐明你隐藏的假设，并从头到尾捍卫你的代码逻辑。

sentences.en: Both of you gain from that friction, and both of you walk away with a significantly better mental model than you started with.
sentences.zh: 你们俩都从这种摩擦中获益，并且你们离开时都拥有比开始时明显更好的心理模型。

blocks.html_en:
Rabbi Ḥama, son of Rabbi Ḥanina, said: What is the meaning of that which is written: “Iron sharpens iron, so a man sharpens the countenance of his friend” (Proverbs 27:17)? This verse comes to tell you that just as with iron blades, one sharpens the other when they are rubbed against each other, so too, when Torah scholars study together, they sharpen one another.

-Ta'anit 7a

sentences.en: Rabbi Ḥama, son of Rabbi Ḥanina, said: What is the meaning of that which is written: “Iron sharpens iron, so a man sharpens the countenance of his friend” (Proverbs 27:17)?
sentences.zh: 哈尼纳拉比的儿子哈马拉比说：“铁能磨铁，人磨朋友的面容”（箴言 27:17）这句话是什么意思？

sentences.en: This verse comes to tell you that just as with iron blades, one sharpens the other when they are rubbed against each other, so too, when Torah scholars study together, they sharpen one another. -Ta'anit 7a
sentences.zh: 这节经文告诉你，就像铁刀一样，当彼此摩擦时，彼此磨利，同样，当托拉学者一起学习时，他们也会彼此磨利。 - 塔阿尼特 7a

blocks.html_en: Rabbi Ḥama, son of Rabbi Ḥanina, said: What is the meaning of that which is written: “Iron sharpens iron, so a man sharpens the countenance of his friend” (Proverbs 27:17)? This verse comes to tell you that just as with iron blades, one sharpens the other when they are rubbed against each other, so too, when Torah scholars study together, they sharpen one another.

sentences.en: Rabbi Ḥama, son of Rabbi Ḥanina, said: What is the meaning of that which is written: “Iron sharpens iron, so a man sharpens the countenance of his friend” (Proverbs 27:17)?
sentences.zh: 哈尼纳拉比的儿子哈马拉比说：“铁能磨铁，人磨朋友的面容”（箴言 27:17）这句话是什么意思？

sentences.en: This verse comes to tell you that just as with iron blades, one sharpens the other when they are rubbed against each other, so too, when Torah scholars study together, they sharpen one another.
sentences.zh: 这节经文告诉你，就像铁刀一样，当彼此摩擦时，彼此磨利，同样，当托拉学者一起学习时，他们也会彼此磨利。

blocks.html_en: -Ta'anit 7a

sentences.en: -Ta'anit 7a
sentences.zh: - 塔阿尼特 7a

blocks.html_en: In our rush for 10x velocity, we tried to replace human pair programming with AI copilots. But in doing so, we forgot a critical dependency: we exchanged the adversarial Chavrusa and Yegiah for a sycophantic robo-duck.

sentences.en: In our rush for 10x velocity, we tried to replace human pair programming with AI copilots.
sentences.zh: 在追求 10 倍速度的过程中，我们尝试用人工智能副驾驶取代人类结对编程。

sentences.en: But in doing so, we forgot a critical dependency: we exchanged the adversarial Chavrusa and Yegiah for a sycophantic robo-duck.
sentences.zh: 但在这样做的过程中，我们忘记了一个关键的依赖关系：我们将敌对的 Chavrusa 和 Yegiah 换成了一只阿谀奉承的机器鸭。

blocks.html_en: Empathetic Balancing Act

sentences.en: Empathetic Balancing Act
sentences.zh: 同理心平衡法

blocks.html_en: A common response I hear from the tech crowd is: "Well, let's just fix this with better system prompts! Let's build guardrails that instruct the AI to act like a Socratic tutor instead of just giving away the answer!"

sentences.en: A common response I hear from the tech crowd is: "Well, let's just fix this with better system prompts!
sentences.zh: 我从技术人员那里听到的一个常见反应是：“好吧，让我们用更好的系统提示来解决这个问题吧！

sentences.en: Let's build guardrails that instruct the AI to act like a Socratic tutor instead of just giving away the answer!"
sentences.zh: 让我们建立护栏，指示人工智能像苏格拉底式导师一样行事，而不是仅仅给出答案！”

blocks.html_en: There are plenty of tools attempting this right now, but I am deeply skeptical that technical guardrails can solve a fundamentally human problem.

sentences.en: There are plenty of tools attempting this right now, but I am deeply skeptical that technical guardrails can solve a fundamentally human problem.
sentences.zh: 目前有很多工具正在尝试这样做，但我对技术护栏能否解决人类的根本问题深表怀疑。

blocks.html_en: First of all, relying on a prompt to withhold answers requires elite learner discipline. Some students can stick to it, but that level of willpower is rare, especially when your cognitive battery is hitting empty.

sentences.en: First of all, relying on a prompt to withhold answers requires elite learner discipline.
sentences.zh: 首先，依靠提示来保留答案需要精英学习者的纪律。

sentences.en: Some students can stick to it, but that level of willpower is rare, especially when your cognitive battery is hitting empty.
sentences.zh: 有些学生可以坚持下去，但这种程度的意志力很少见，尤其是当你的认知电池耗尽时。

blocks.html_en: When a junior developer is staring at a cryptic NullPointerException at 2 AM and their brain is fried, a prompt asking them to "reflect on the lifecycle of the object" is getting bypassed immediately. If all it takes is typing /solution or opening a new browser tab to get the dopamine hit of copypastable code, not too many people I know will power through.

sentences.en: When a junior developer is staring at a cryptic NullPointerException at 2 AM and their brain is fried, a prompt asking them to "reflect on the lifecycle of the object" is getting bypassed immediately.
sentences.zh: 当一名初级开发人员在凌晨 2 点盯着一个神秘的 NullPointerException 时，他们的大脑陷入了困境，要求他们“反思对象生命周期”的提示会立即被绕过。

sentences.en: If all it takes is typing /solution or opening a new browser tab to get the dopamine hit of copypastable code, not too many people I know will power through.
sentences.zh: 如果只需输入 /solution 或打开一个新的浏览器选项卡即可获得可复制代码的多巴胺刺激，那么我认识的人中没有多少人会通过。

blocks.html_en: More importantly, LLMs are fundamentally architected to please. Even if you prompt-engineer them to be tough Socratic tutors, their core tuning means that the moment a user expresses genuine frustration, the AI folds like a cheap lawn chair and hands over the syntax anyway.

sentences.en: More importantly, LLMs are fundamentally architected to please.
sentences.zh: 更重要的是，法学硕士从根本上就是为了取悦他人而设计的。

sentences.en: Even if you prompt-engineer them to be tough Socratic tutors, their core tuning means that the moment a user expresses genuine frustration, the AI folds like a cheap lawn chair and hands over the syntax anyway.
sentences.zh: 即使你将它们设计成强硬的苏格拉底式导师，它们的核心调整也意味着，当用户表达出真正的挫败感时，人工智能就会像廉价的草坪椅一样折叠起来，并无论如何都会交出语法。

blocks.html_en: Why? Because an LLM lacks the quality most important in an educator: empathy.

sentences.en: Why?
sentences.zh: 为什么？

sentences.en: Because an LLM lacks the quality most important in an educator: empathy.
sentences.zh: 因为法学硕士缺乏教育者最重要的品质：同理心。

blocks.html_en: Mentorship is a human pedagogical balancing act. A great mentor understands the fine line between productive struggle and demoralizing suffering. A good educator can read the room. They notice the heavy sighs, the mounting panic, and the sheer exhaustion.

sentences.en: Mentorship is a human pedagogical balancing act.
sentences.zh: 指导是一种人类教学平衡行为。

sentences.en: A great mentor understands the fine line between productive struggle and demoralizing suffering.
sentences.zh: 一位伟大的导师了解富有成效的斗争和令人沮丧的痛苦之间的微妙界限。

sentences.en: A good educator can read the room.
sentences.zh: 一个好的教育家可以阅读房间。

sentences.en: They notice the heavy sighs, the mounting panic, and the sheer exhaustion.
sentences.zh: 他们注意到沉重的叹息、日益加剧的恐慌和彻底的疲惫。

blocks.html_en: Sure, as a student I learned best when I was given the tools to figure things out on my own. But sometimes I maxed out my energy on the stupid error message, and all of the leading questions my tutors were asking me felt like they were just causing me to circle and circle around the one insight that would make it all work, an insight my brain just couldn't reach.

sentences.en: Sure, as a student I learned best when I was given the tools to figure things out on my own.
sentences.zh: 当然，作为一名学生，当我拥有自己解决问题的工具时，我学得最好。

sentences.en: But sometimes I maxed out my energy on the stupid error message, and all of the leading questions my tutors were asking me felt like they were just causing me to circle and circle around the one insight that would make it all work, an insight my brain just couldn't reach.
sentences.zh: 但有时我在愚蠢的错误信息上耗尽了我的精力，我的导师问我的所有主要问题都让我觉得它们只是让我围绕着一个能让一切顺利进行的洞察力，一个我的大脑无法达到的洞察力。

blocks.html_en: That is the point a good teacher knows to step back, smile, say "Hey, JavaScript is just weird sometimes," and hand the exact line of code I needed to get unstuck so I could finally go get some sleep.

sentences.en: That is the point a good teacher knows to step back, smile, say "Hey, JavaScript is just weird sometimes," and hand the exact line of code I needed to get unstuck so I could finally go get some sleep.
sentences.zh: 这就是一位好老师知道的一点，退后一步，微笑着说“嘿，JavaScript 有时很奇怪”，然后递给我需要摆脱困境的确切代码行，这样我终于可以去睡觉了。

blocks.html_en: An algorithm cannot read a learner's emotional state. It cannot dynamically toggle between a challenging sparring partner and a supportive safety net. Code can never replicate that relationship.

sentences.en: An algorithm cannot read a learner's emotional state.
sentences.zh: 算法无法读取学习者的情绪状态。

sentences.en: It cannot dynamically toggle between a challenging sparring partner and a supportive safety net.
sentences.zh: 它无法在具有挑战性的陪练伙伴和支持性安全网之间动态切换。

sentences.en: Code can never replicate that relationship.
sentences.zh: 代码永远无法复制这种关系。

blocks.html_en: The Unsolved Catch-22

sentences.en: The Unsolved Catch-22
sentences.zh: 未解决的第 22 条军规

blocks.html_en: So where does this leave us? If I’m being entirely honest, I have way more questions than answers right now (not exactly what you want to hear from a tech blog, I know!).

sentences.en: So where does this leave us?
sentences.zh: 那么这会给我们带来什么影响呢？

sentences.en: If I’m being entirely honest, I have way more questions than answers right now (not exactly what you want to hear from a tech blog, I know!).
sentences.zh: 如果我完全诚实的话，我现在的问题比答案多得多（我知道这不完全是您想从技术博客中听到的内容！）。

blocks.html_en: We are sitting on a massive, industry-wide Catch-22:

sentences.en: We are sitting on a massive, industry-wide Catch-22:
sentences.zh: 我们正面临着一个巨大的、全行业范围内的第二十二条军规：

blocks.html_en: On one hand, it would be borderline professional malpractice to forbid junior developers from using AI. It is the modern IDE (which, back when I was learning to code, gatekeepers argued juniors shouldn't use either—I disagreed then and I disagree now). If juniors will be expected to use these tools on the job, it hardly makes sense not to train them to use them effectively.

sentences.en: On one hand, it would be borderline professional malpractice to forbid junior developers from using AI.
sentences.zh: 一方面，禁止初级开发人员使用人工智能将是近乎职业渎职的行为。

sentences.en: It is the modern IDE (which, back when I was learning to code, gatekeepers argued juniors shouldn't use either—I disagreed then and I disagree now).
sentences.zh: 它是现代 IDE（当我学习编码时，守门人认为初级人员不应该使用它——我当时不同意，现在也不同意）。

sentences.en: If juniors will be expected to use these tools on the job, it hardly makes sense not to train them to use them effectively.
sentences.zh: 如果初级员工被期望在工作中使用这些工具，那么不培训他们有效地使用它们就没有意义。

blocks.html_en: On the other hand, the engineers getting that massive 10x velocity boost without introducing architecture-destroying bugs are the seniors who spent years writing code without it.

sentences.en: On the other hand, the engineers getting that massive 10x velocity boost without introducing architecture-destroying bugs are the seniors who spent years writing code without it.
sentences.zh: 另一方面，那些在没有引入破坏架构的错误的情况下获得了 10 倍巨大速度提升的工程师是那些花了数年时间编写没有它的代码的资深工程师。

blocks.html_en: We know when an LLM is spinning its wheels on a false premise because we have a built-in BS-meter, hard-earned through years of manual debugging.

sentences.en: We know when an LLM is spinning its wheels on a false premise because we have a built-in BS-meter, hard-earned through years of manual debugging.
sentences.zh: 我们知道法学硕士何时会在错误的前提下白费力气，因为我们有一个内置的 BS 计，这是通过多年的手动调试来之不易的。

blocks.html_en: How does someone without that pre-AI experience build that intuition today? They won't get it from a sycophantic yes-man chat-bot assistant. And manually inventing your own cognitive friction requires a level of self-discipline that is hard to maintain on a tight deadline.

sentences.en: How does someone without that pre-AI experience build that intuition today?
sentences.zh: 没有人工智能之前的经验的人今天如何建立这种直觉？

sentences.en: They won't get it from a sycophantic yes-man chat-bot assistant.
sentences.zh: 他们不会从一个阿谀奉承、唯唯诺诺的聊天机器人助理那里得到它。

sentences.en: And manually inventing your own cognitive friction requires a level of self-discipline that is hard to maintain on a tight deadline.
sentences.zh: 手动发明自己的认知摩擦需要一定程度的自律，而在紧迫的期限内很难维持这种自律。

blocks.html_en: Maybe the future of onboarding relies on doubling down on human pair programming—having juniors shadow seniors for months on end to soak up that pre-AI intuition by proxy. After all, pair programming has always been one of the best onboarding methods, even before Claude. But not all companies are interested in the investment that full-time pairing requires in an industry often obsessed with shipping yesterday.

sentences.en: Maybe the future of onboarding relies on doubling down on human pair programming—having juniors shadow seniors for months on end to soak up that pre-AI intuition by proxy.
sentences.zh: 也许入职培训的未来依赖于加倍的人类结对编程——让初级人员连续几个月跟随高级人员，通过代理吸收人工智能出现之前的直觉。

sentences.en: After all, pair programming has always been one of the best onboarding methods, even before Claude.
sentences.zh: 毕竟，结对编程一直是最好的入职方法之一，甚至在克劳德之前也是如此。

sentences.en: But not all companies are interested in the investment that full-time pairing requires in an industry often obsessed with shipping yesterday.
sentences.zh: 但并非所有公司都对过去常常沉迷于运输的行业中全职配对所需的投资感兴趣。

blocks.html_en: What I do know is that we cannot afford to ignore the problem or assume that better LLM models will magically fix our talent pipeline.

sentences.en: What I do know is that we cannot afford to ignore the problem or assume that better LLM models will magically fix our talent pipeline.
sentences.zh: 我所知道的是，我们不能忽视这个问题，也不能认为更好的法学硕士模型会神奇地修复我们的人才管道。

blocks.html_en: If we just hand junior developers an oracle and fail to preserve the empathetic, rigorous struggle of the Chavrusa, we are going to wake up in a few years to a terrifying system error: an industry with a massive, AI-generated codebase to maintain, and not enough senior engineers left who know how to maintain it.

sentences.en: If we just hand junior developers an oracle and fail to preserve the empathetic, rigorous struggle of the Chavrusa, we are going to wake up in a few years to a terrifying system error: an industry with a massive, AI-generated codebase to maintain, and not enough senior engineers left who know how to maintain it.
sentences.zh: 如果我们只是向初级开发人员提供一个预言，而未能保留 Chavrusa 的同理心、严谨的斗争，几年后我们就会发现一个可怕的系统错误：这个行业需要维护大量由人工智能生成的代码库，而没有足够的高级工程师知道如何维护它。

blocks.html_en: What are you and your teams doing to keep productive friction alive? I'd love to hear your thoughts.

sentences.en: What are you and your teams doing to keep productive friction alive?
sentences.zh: 您和您的团队正在采取哪些措施来保持富有成效的摩擦？

sentences.en: I'd love to hear your thoughts.
sentences.zh: 我很想听听你的想法。

content_html_zh:
那些关注我一段时间的人会知道教学和指导对我来说是一件大事™️。

在我进入科技行业之前，我是一名教师，并且仍然认为自己本质上是一名教师。 在我眼中，成为一名教师和导师与成为一名优秀的程序员和工程师是分不开的。相反，教学是一种工具，可以帮助我在旅程的每个阶段提高自己的技能。

但在过去几年中，并且在过去几个月中，教学环境正在以惊人的速度发生变化。 法学硕士和“人工智能”编码助理的出现极大地改变了我们获取工程技能的方式，而这是我们绝对没有准备好的。

所有这些都引发了许多想法和对话，我希望在这篇博文中提炼出其中的一些。

按照真正的塔木德风格，这篇文章不包含太多答案，但希望会给您留下比开始时更多的问题。 但提出问题是我们开始这些对话的方式，如果我们要让下一代程序员和工程师做正确的事，就需要进行这些对话。

别用勺子喂我

作为一名学生，无论是在 Yeshiva 每天花几个小时钻研密集的塔木德法律辩论和深奥的 Chassisdic 哲学，还是后来在 Flatiron 训练营学习 Rails 和 React 时，我很快就意识到了关于技能获取的一个令人不安的事实。

我最好、最深刻的学习从来都不是在课程顺利的时候发生的；当我痛苦地陷入困境时，我的头撞在一个神秘的错误消息上，或者与一个根本无法理解的概念搏斗时（通常是在凌晨 2 点，由冷咖啡和纯粹的固执推动）。

当你消除这种挣扎时，你就摆脱了成长。 当你摆脱成长时，学习就不会发生。

即使你的记忆力只够通过考试，“来得容易去得也快”。 如果没有这种认知摩擦，当你关闭编辑器时，知识就会消失。

我所见过的最好的老师和导师直观地理解了这一点。 当我盯着一个损坏的脚本，知道可能有一个简单到愚蠢的修复方法时，他们并没有突然冲进来，把解决方案放到我的屏幕上（这让我睡眠不足的大脑发出了沮丧的声音）。

相反，他们使用苏格拉底方法等工具。 他们会指出某行特定的代码，并开始提出温和的、探究性的问题，以揭露我有缺陷的假设。

他们积极地利用摩擦来创造足够的认知空间，让我自己把这些点联系起来。

他们了解我们教学技巧的一条基本规则：良好的指导并不是要了解代码。 今天的目标不仅仅是让测试变绿；这是为了建立解决明天下一个不可避免的故障所需的心理弹性。

我所掌握的正是这些技能。 当轮到我成为老师时，无论是在犹太在线学校教二年级学生，在熨斗公司担任助教，还是在指导加入我的团队的新队友时。

IDE 中的唯唯诺诺的人

快进到今天，我们的行业已将最初的指导层很大程度上外包给了法学硕士和编码助理。 这是一个令人不安的事实：人工智能会成为一个糟糕的苏格拉底式导师。

如果你认真地花时间与现代法学硕士结对（甚至只是聊天），你就会知道他们是病态的唯唯诺诺的人。 告诉人工智能你想为一个有 12 个活跃用户的待办事项列表应用程序构建一个分布式微服务网格，它不会拒绝。 它将附和“这是一个绝妙的见解！” 并立即吐出 400 行样板文件。

人工智能默认是盲目信任。 它很少挑战错误的假设，当你问它为什么堆栈跟踪会崩溃时，它会立即提供可复制粘贴的修复程序，而不是帮助你理解潜在的错误。

通过像算法自动售货机（error_in => fix_out）一样，人工智能系统地消除了开发过程中的所有认知摩擦。

在我们追求 10 倍速度的过程中，我们忘记了技能获取的基本规则：摩擦是一种功能，而不是错误。 那是我们移除的承重摩擦！

这不仅仅是哲学推测；有硬数据支持它。 Anthropic 最近的一项研究（如果您喜欢解析方法，请深入了解这里的原始论文）让 52 名软件工程师学习一个不熟悉的编程库，并将他们分成两组：一组使用 AI 编码助手，一组手动编码。

总体结果既令人震惊，又不足为奇。 将编码委托给 AI 的工程师的概念掌握和调试技能下降了 17%（相当于两个完整的字母等级！） 通常无法比手动对照组更快地完成任务。

为什么？ 因为当你摆脱了痛苦地卡住的认知摩擦时，你的大脑永远不会构建评估生成的代码是否真的有任何好处所需的深层神经通路。

现在，公平地说，数据中存在一些细微差别：少数人工智能辅助工程师的表现实际上优于手动控制组。 但他们是谁？ 他们是拒绝盲目相信人工智能的开发者。 他们像对待学习伙伴一样对待法学硕士，提出探究性的后续问题，并手动将自己的摩擦注入到这个过程中。

对于其他人来说，自动化的唯唯诺诺的人只是悄悄地破坏了他们的理解。

学习者的困境：手动注入摩擦力

这种动态让初级开发人员陷入了巨大的困境。

职业生涯早期的朋友和学员抱怨说，他们感觉陷入了令人沮丧的第 22 条军规：

一方面，他们必须务实地接受人工智能不会消失的现实。 如果法学硕士可以通过单个提示生成工作样板，那么您就不能固执地不使用它。 考虑到您将在日常工作流程中大量使用这些工具，忽略人工智能完全让人感觉不负责任。

但另一方面，如果你盲目依赖自动完成功能，你就会陷入所谓的“肤浅学习”。 您会得到一个快速答案，允许您通过测试套件或获得 PR 批准，而无需真正了解底层机制。 你错过了“深度学习”，而真正的、职业维持性的成长正是在“深度学习”中发生的。

根据我的经验，这种肤浅是导致开发人员焦虑和冒名顶替综合症的直接途径。 真正的职业信心不是来自于按 Tab 键接受提示；而是来自于按 Tab 键接受提示。它来自代码所有权和大量的智力劳动和汗水。

为了生存下去，勤奋的学习者现在被迫手动将摩擦注入到他们的工作流程中，以防止他们的大脑关闭。

我的一位初级开发人员朋友使用护栏指令明确提示他们的 IDE，例如：“我想学习如何自己编码，而不是复制和粘贴，因此请尽可能启用它。”

他们用手记笔记（是的，真正的纸和笔！） 并设置物理计时器只是为了重新创建积极倾听的保障，而为即时满足而设计的工具会系统地试图绕过这一保障。

为什么我们需要混乱的上下文网络

当你读一下 Emily Bender 和 Alex Hanna 的书《The AI Con》时，这种自我斗争的需要就完全有意义了，这本书强调了为什么信息访问中的摩擦实际上是一个功能，而不是一个错误。

想想当您使用搜索引擎手动寻找一个不起眼的错误时会发生什么。

你阅读凌乱的文档，浏览 12 个评论的 GitHub 问题线程（通常以维护者在 2014 年将其关闭为“不是错误”而结束），权衡 Stack Overflow 上相互冲突的答案的可信度，并一路上偶然发现相关的问题。

混乱的元信息网络是构建真正的工程环境的地方。

人工智能聊天机器人将所有丰富的、令人沮丧的斗争简化为一个听起来很权威（有时是“幻觉”）的摘要。 它为你提供了当前问题的答案，同时剥夺了你的大脑解决下一个问题所需的周围生态系统知识。

Chavrusa 模型：结对编程和对抗性橡皮鸭

这让我想到了一种古老的教学模型，该模型至今仍在世界各地的 Yeshivas（塔木德学院）中幸存，也是软件工程现在迫切需要的模型。

在传统的犹太学院中，你很少看到人们安静地坐着看书或被动地听讲座。 相反，房间里很吵闹，充满活力，充满了成双成对地激烈争吵的人。

这个学习伙伴系统被称为 Chavrusa（源自亚拉姆语，意为“朋友”或“同伴”）。

当你和 Chavrusa 一起学习时，你的搭档不会在那里读课文给你听，也不会点头同意并阿谀奉承地肯定你的理论有多么出色。 真正的查夫鲁萨经常挑战你的逻辑，在你的论点中找出漏洞，反对不可靠的假设，并且通常拒绝让你摆脱智力上的懒惰。

你不能通过阅读悬崖笔记或听精彩的讲座来掌握《塔木德》（尽管也有一些这样的方法）；你可以通过 Yegiah（希伯来语术语，翻译过来就是大量的智力劳动和汗水）来掌握它。

如果你仔细想想（正如我在 2021 年 RailsConf 演讲中提到的），结对编程只是科技行业对 Chavrusa 的再创造。

这是该系统中最重要的部分：不仅仅是两人中的初级成员在真正的 Chavrusa 中学习。

无论您是与工作 10 年的资深工程师还是刚刚完成训练营的初级开发人员结对，都没关系；学习永远是一条双向路！

当你的伙伴扮演一只敌对的橡皮鸭（实际上是顶嘴的橡皮鸭！）时，他们会迫使你放慢速度，阐明你隐藏的假设，并从头到尾捍卫你的代码逻辑。 你们俩都从这种摩擦中获益，并且你们离开时都拥有比开始时明显更好的心理模型。

哈尼纳拉比的儿子哈马拉比说：“铁能磨铁，人磨朋友的面容”（箴言 27:17）这句话是什么意思？ 这节经文告诉你，就像铁刀一样，当彼此摩擦时，彼此磨利，同样，当托拉学者一起学习时，他们也会彼此磨利。 - 塔阿尼特 7a

在追求 10 倍速度的过程中，我们尝试用人工智能副驾驶取代人类结对编程。 但在这样做的过程中，我们忘记了一个关键的依赖关系：我们将敌对的 Chavrusa 和 Yegiah 换成了一只阿谀奉承的机器鸭。

同理心平衡法

我从技术人员那里听到的一个常见反应是：“好吧，让我们用更好的系统提示来解决这个问题吧！ 让我们建立护栏，指示人工智能像苏格拉底式导师一样行事，而不是仅仅给出答案！”

目前有很多工具正在尝试这样做，但我对技术护栏能否解决人类的根本问题深表怀疑。

首先，依靠提示来保留答案需要精英学习者的纪律。 有些学生可以坚持下去，但这种程度的意志力很少见，尤其是当你的认知电池耗尽时。

当一名初级开发人员在凌晨 2 点盯着一个神秘的 NullPointerException 时，他们的大脑陷入了困境，要求他们“反思对象生命周期”的提示会立即被绕过。 如果只需输入 /solution 或打开一个新的浏览器选项卡即可获得可复制代码的多巴胺刺激，那么我认识的人中没有多少人会通过。

更重要的是，法学硕士从根本上就是为了取悦他人而设计的。 即使你将它们设计成强硬的苏格拉底式导师，它们的核心调整也意味着，当用户表达出真正的挫败感时，人工智能就会像廉价的草坪椅一样折叠起来，并无论如何都会交出语法。

为什么？ 因为法学硕士缺乏教育者最重要的品质：同理心。

指导是一种人类教学平衡行为。 一位伟大的导师了解富有成效的斗争和令人沮丧的痛苦之间的微妙界限。 一个好的教育家可以阅读房间。 他们注意到沉重的叹息、日益加剧的恐慌和彻底的疲惫。

当然，作为一名学生，当我拥有自己解决问题的工具时，我学得最好。 但有时我在愚蠢的错误信息上耗尽了我的精力，我的导师问我的所有主要问题都让我觉得它们只是让我围绕着一个能让一切顺利进行的洞察力，一个我的大脑无法达到的洞察力。

这就是一位好老师知道的一点，退后一步，微笑着说“嘿，JavaScript 有时很奇怪”，然后递给我需要摆脱困境的确切代码行，这样我终于可以去睡觉了。

算法无法读取学习者的情绪状态。 它无法在具有挑战性的陪练伙伴和支持性安全网之间动态切换。 代码永远无法复制这种关系。

未解决的第 22 条军规

那么这会给我们带来什么影响呢？ 如果我完全诚实的话，我现在的问题比答案多得多（我知道这不完全是您想从技术博客中听到的内容！）。

我们正面临着一个巨大的、全行业范围内的第二十二条军规：

一方面，禁止初级开发人员使用人工智能将是近乎职业渎职的行为。 它是现代 IDE（当我学习编码时，守门人认为初级人员不应该使用它——我当时不同意，现在也不同意）。 如果初级员工被期望在工作中使用这些工具，那么不培训他们有效地使用它们就没有意义。

另一方面，那些在没有引入破坏架构的错误的情况下获得了 10 倍巨大速度提升的工程师是那些花了数年时间编写没有它的代码的资深工程师。

我们知道法学硕士何时会在错误的前提下白费力气，因为我们有一个内置的 BS 计，这是通过多年的手动调试来之不易的。

没有人工智能之前的经验的人今天如何建立这种直觉？ 他们不会从一个阿谀奉承、唯唯诺诺的聊天机器人助理那里得到它。 手动发明自己的认知摩擦需要一定程度的自律，而在紧迫的期限内很难维持这种自律。

也许入职培训的未来依赖于加倍的人类结对编程——让初级人员连续几个月跟随高级人员，通过代理吸收人工智能出现之前的直觉。 毕竟，结对编程一直是最好的入职方法之一，甚至在克劳德之前也是如此。 但并非所有公司都对过去常常沉迷于运输的行业中全职配对所需的投资感兴趣。

我所知道的是，我们不能忽视这个问题，也不能认为更好的法学硕士模型会神奇地修复我们的人才管道。

如果我们只是向初级开发人员提供一个预言，而未能保留 Chavrusa 的同理心、严谨的斗争，几年后我们就会发现一个可怕的系统错误：这个行业需要维护大量由人工智能生成的代码库，而没有足够的高级工程师知道如何维护它。

您和您的团队正在采取哪些措施来保持富有成效的摩擦？ 我很想听听你的想法。
